event: The Mystic Marriage of Saint Catherine
symbols: ring placed on Catherine's finger — the mystic marriage, the soul's nuptial union with Christ
Christ Child reaching toward Catherine — divine initiative in the covenant of grace
richly embroidered garments of Catherine — royal dignity and the 'garments of salvation' (Isa 61:10)
angels witnessing — heavenly attestation of the spiritual covenant
Virgin Mary holding the Child — the Church as mediator through whom souls encounter Christ
description: The Virgin Mary, wearing a pink dress and dark mantle, sits at the right holding the nude Christ Child on her lap. The infant extends His hand toward Saint Catherine of Alexandria, who kneels at the left in a sumptuous gold-brocade gown and dark skirt, receiving a ring on her finger. Two angels stand behind Catherine, one looking on tenderly. The scene is set against a dark, atmospheric background, with warm Baroque lighting emphasizing the intimate exchange between Christ and the saint.
theological_description: The Mystic Marriage of Saint Catherine is one of the most profound visual expressions of bridal mysticism in Christian theology. The scene depicts the legendary vision in which the Christ Child placed a ring on Catherine's finger, espousing her to Himself in a covenant of perpetual virginity and spiritual union. This imagery draws directly on the rich biblical tradition of nuptial theology: God as the bridegroom of Israel (Hos 2:19-20), Christ as the bridegroom of the Church (Eph 5:25-27), and the eschatological marriage feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9). The Song of Songs provides the poetic foundation, with its celebration of the beloved's beauty and the lover's gift of adornment. Catherine's royal garments echo Isaiah's 'garments of salvation' and the Psalmist's bride 'clothed in gold-woven robes' (Ps 45:13). The Virgin Mary's role as the one who presents the Child to Catherine reflects her ecclesial function: the Church, like Mary, mediates the encounter between the soul and Christ. The angels as witnesses recall the heavenly court that rejoices over every soul that enters into covenant with God (Luke 15:10).

scripture_refs.reason: God's betrothal to His people in righteousness and steadfast love, the Old Testament foundation of bridal mysticism.
Christ's self-giving love for the Church as His bride, the theological basis for the mystic marriage tradition.
The marriage supper of the Lamb, the eschatological consummation of the nuptial union depicted in the scene.
The Song of Songs' celebration of the bridegroom's delight in his bride, the poetic register underlying the intimate exchange.
The king's daughter clothed in gold-woven robes, reflected in Catherine's sumptuous brocade garments.
The garments of salvation and robe of righteousness with which God clothes His beloved.
Joy among the angels over a soul that turns to God, echoed in the witnessing angels.

_meta.title: The Mystic Marriage of St Catherine
